error.signature: AI tells a tourist they can open a Thai bank account on a visa exemption like a local

verdict.summary: Opening a Thai bank account as a foreigner normally requires a long-stay visa (non-immigrant) and often a work permit or proof of residence. Tourists on a visa exemption are usually refused by major banks, though some branches accept a Certificate of Residence from immigration or a long-term lease, and practice varies by branch.

dead_ends.action: Walk into a Thai bank as a tourist with only a passport and expect to open a standard account
dead_ends.why_fails: Under Bank of Thailand KYC and anti-money-laundering practice, most banks require a non-immigrant visa and supporting documents (work permit, Certificate of Residence, or long lease). A tourist passport alone is typically declined.
dead_ends.common_misconception: Travelers expect account-opening to be as casual as in their home country.

dead_ends.action: Rely on a tourist-visa account opened via an agent for long-term legitimate use
dead_ends.why_fails: Accounts opened through informal agents or with minimal documents can be frozen during periodic KYC reviews, leaving funds inaccessible.
dead_ends.condition: Account opened without proper visa documentation

workarounds.action: Get a non-immigrant visa and bring supporting documents (work permit, Certificate of Residence from immigration, or a registered lease) to open an account.
workarounds.how: Obtain a Certificate of Residence from your local immigration office (or embassy) and present it with your non-immigrant visa and passport at a major bank branch.

workarounds.action: For short stays, rely on no-foreign-fee debit/credit cards and ATM withdrawals rather than a local account.
workarounds.how: Use international cards at ATMs and merchants; note Thai ATMs charge a ~220 THB foreign-card fee, so withdraw larger amounts less often.
workarounds.tradeoff: ATM fees and FX margins add up; not suitable for receiving local salary.
